title: Pardubice Region

([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *Pardubický kraj*) is an administrative unit ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *kraj*) of the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), located mainly in the eastern part of its historical region of [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia"), with a small part in northwestern [Moravia](/wiki/Moravia "Moravia"). It is named after its capital [Pardubice](/wiki/Pardubice "Pardubice"). As an administrative unit, Pardubice Region has existed three times in the course of history. It was established for the first time in 1850, and extended from [Český Brod](/wiki/%C4%8Cesk%C3%BD_Brod "Český Brod") to the Bohemian-Moravian border. In its second existence, it was one of 19 regions as they were set between 1949 and 1960. After 1960, Pardubice became the capital of Pardubice district, which was part of the Eastern Bohemian Region (capital [Hradec Králové](/wiki/Hradec_Kr%C3%A1lov%C3%A9 "Hradec Králové")). The Pardubice Region, as it is now, was reestablished in 2000.

**Pardubice Region** ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *Pardubický kraj*) is an administrative unit ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *kraj*) of the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), located mainly in the eastern part of its historical region of [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia"), with a small part in northwestern [Moravia](/wiki/Moravia "Moravia"). It is named after its capital [Pardubice](/wiki/Pardubice "Pardubice"). As an administrative unit, Pardubice Region has existed three times in the course of history. It was established for the first time in 1850, and extended from [Český Brod](/wiki/%C4%8Cesk%C3%BD_Brod "Český Brod") to the Bohemian-Moravian border. In its second existence, it was one of 19 regions as they were set between 1949 and 1960. After 1960, Pardubice became the capital of Pardubice district, which was part of the Eastern Bohemian Region (capital [Hradec Králové](/wiki/Hradec_Kr%C3%A1lov%C3%A9 "Hradec Králové")). The Pardubice Region, as it is now, was reestablished in 2000.

## Administrative divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pardubice_Region&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Administrative divisions")]

The Pardubice Region is divided into 4 districts:

|  |  |

There are a total of 451 municipalities in the region (as of 2019).[[3]](#cite_note-csu-3) Among these are 15 municipalities with extended powers and 26 municipalities with a delegated municipal office. Thirty-two of the municipalities are classified as towns. The regional central offices are in Pardubice.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pardubice_Region&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Population")]

The total population of the Pardubice Region is about 530,000. 17 municipalities have a higher population than 5,000. The largest municipality of the region is [Pardubice](/wiki/Pardubice "Pardubice") with a population of more than 90,000. The table below shows the municipalities in Pardubice Region with the largest population (as of 1 January 2024).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pardubice_Region&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

With a total size of 4,519 km2, Pardubice is the fifth smallest region in the Czech Republic. [Králický Sněžník](/wiki/Kr%C3%A1lick%C3%BD_Sn%C4%9B%C5%BEn%C3%ADk "Králický Sněžník") (1,424 m) is the highest point in the region. The lowest point (201 m) is situated on the water surface of the [Elbe](/wiki/Elbe "Elbe") River near Kojice.

The southern and southeastern parts of the region are home to the hilly areas of [Upper Svratka Highlands](/wiki/Upper_Svratka_Highlands "Upper Svratka Highlands") and [Iron Mountains](/wiki/Iron_Mountains_(Czech_Republic) "Iron Mountains (Czech Republic)"). The central and western parts of the region are formed by the [Polabí](/wiki/Polab%C3%AD "Polabí") lowlands. In the northeast, the region reaches the [Orlické Mountains](/wiki/Orlick%C3%A9_Mountains "Orlické Mountains") and [Hrubý Jeseník](/wiki/Hrub%C3%BD_Jesen%C3%ADk "Hrubý Jeseník") range.

The [European Watershed](/wiki/European_Watershed "European Watershed") runs through the region, where it separates the basins of the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") and the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"). The tripoint of the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") (Black Sea), [Elbe](/wiki/Elbe "Elbe") (North Sea) and [Oder](/wiki/Oder "Oder") ([Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea")) watersheds is located at the peak of [Králický Sněžník](/wiki/Kr%C3%A1lick%C3%BD_Sn%C4%9B%C5%BEn%C3%ADk "Králický Sněžník"). The majority of the region belongs to the [drainage basin](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") of the Elbe, consisting of the rivers [Chrudimka](/wiki/Chrudimka "Chrudimka"), [Divoká Orlice](/wiki/Divok%C3%A1_Orlice "Divoká Orlice"), Doubrava, Loučná, [Tichá Orlice](/wiki/Tich%C3%A1_Orlice "Tichá Orlice") and Třebovka. The rivers in the drainage basin of the Danube are the Morava, Moravská Sázava, Svitava and Třebůvka. The [Seč Reservoir](/wiki/Se%C4%8D_Reservoir "Seč Reservoir") (220 ha) and the Pastviny Reservoir (92 ha) are among the larger water bodies in the region.

The most prevalent soil types in the region are [podzol](/wiki/Podzol "Podzol") and [cambisol](/wiki/Cambisol "Cambisol"). Forests cover 29.6% of the region and are predominantly [coniferous](/wiki/Coniferous "Coniferous"). [Deciduous](/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous") forests occur around Pardubice.

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pardubice_Region&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

Pardubice Region has a continental climate characterized by relatively hot summers and cold winters. The climate in Pardubice Region differs depending on location. The areas in the [Polabí](/wiki/Polab%C3%AD "Polabí") lowlands in the western part of the region are relatively warmer with annual mean temperatures reaching 8 °C. On the other hand, the coldest areas are situated in the hilly northeastern part of the region, where the annual mean temperatures are around 4 °C.

Precipitation is relatively lower in the central part of the region where the total annual values are around 700 – 800 mm. In the areas with higher elevation (such as Žďárské Vrchy or the foothills of Orlické Hory) there is higher precipitation with annual values of 800 – 1,000 mm.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pardubice_Region&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Economy")]

The [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP) per capita of the region was 81.1% of the national average in 2011. Services form the largest part of the region's economy, forming 52.4% of GDP in 2011. Other significant sectors are industry (36.9% of GDP) and construction (7.2% of GDP). The registered [unemployment rate](/wiki/Unemployment_rate "Unemployment rate") was 8.44% at the end of 2011. The highest unemployment rate was in [Svitavy District](/wiki/Svitavy_District "Svitavy District").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Paramo_Pardubice_1.JPG/250px-Paramo_Pardubice_1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Paramo_Pardubice_1.JPG)

Industry in Pardubice

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Foxconn_Pardubice.JPG/250px-Foxconn_Pardubice.JPG)](/wiki/File:Foxconn_Pardubice.JPG)

Foxconn in Pardubice (formerly Tesla Pardubice)

* **Industry**

Industry and commercial public services are the basis of Pardubice's economy. General engineering, textiles, clothing, and leather processing are the strongest industrial branches. The chemical industry in Pardubice Region is larger than anywhere else in the Czech Republic.

* **Transport**

Road networks make up 3,597 km that transverse throughout the region. Roads I/35 and I/37 (connecting to I/17 in Chrudim) are noted as the most important. A 9 km section of the [D11 motorway](/wiki/D11_motorway_(Czech_Republic) "D11 motorway (Czech Republic)") connect the region to Prague. The two-lane [R35 expressway](/wiki/R35_expressway_(Czech_Republic) "R35 expressway (Czech Republic)") is planned to cross the region and connect it with Liberec, Germany and the Moravian network of highways and expressways.

There are 542 km of railways in the region, connecting it to Prague, Brno, Liberec, Olomouc, Ostrava, and to Poland. The railway corridor of Berlin – Prague – Brno – Vienna crosses the region and in Pardubice it is connected to routes of national significance to Liberec and Havlíčkův Brod.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Pardubice has an [international airport](/wiki/Pardubice_Airport "Pardubice Airport"), operating for both public and military flights, which is listed in the category of the top 5 airports in the Czech Republic.

* **Agriculture**

Agriculture plays an important role in the region. The agricultural land covers about 60.2% of all land in the region, while the [arable land](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land") occupies 43.8%. Forests cover 29.6% of the region.

* **Tourism**

Attractions from natural tourist spots (such as the Železné Hory and the Orlické Hory) to historical monuments, mostly medieval castles established since the 13th century, all encourage tourism to the region. The most significant historical monuments in the region are connected with the noble Pernštejn family from Moravia, who had two huge castles built, at [Litice nad Orlicí](/wiki/Litice_nad_Orlic%C3%AD "Litice nad Orlicí"), and on Kunětická hora near Pardubice.[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 2011 there were 365 facilities providing accommodation in the region for 324,000 visitors.

## Education and health care

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pardubice_Region&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Education and health care")]

In 2011 there were:

* 309 kindergartens with 17,994 students
* 251 elementary schools with 41,184 students
* 21 secondary elementary schools (Czech: *[gymnázium](/wiki/Gymnasium_(school) "Gymnasium (school)")*) with 6,305 students
* 55 high schools with 16,413 students
* 10 colleges (Czech: *vyšší odborná škola*) with 1,135 students
* [University of Pardubice](/wiki/University_of_Pardubice "University of Pardubice") with seven faculties and more than 10,000 students.

Furthermore, there were nine hospitals with a capacity of 2,584 patients. In total, there were 2,009 doctors in the region and the patient-per-doctor ratio was 257.
